entries.intro: I'm proud to call {settlement_name} home. It's not perfect, but it's ours.
details: We built most of what you see here with our own hands. Every building has a story.
It's quiet, it's safe enough, and the people look out for each other. What more do you need?
reactions: I've thought about leaving, sure. But I always come back to the same answer.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} could use some work, if I'm being honest.
details: Half the fences need mending and the paths turn to mud the second it rains.
It's not that nobody cares. Everyone's just busy keeping their own place together.
reactions: It's home, though. For better or worse.

statCheck.pass: The real problem is drainage. The ground slopes the wrong way and nobody planned for it when they laid the paths.
statCheck.fail: I'm no builder. I just know it looks rough.

entries.intro: I like how {settlement_name} feels at dusk. Everything quiets down and the light turns golden.
details: People finish up their work and you can hear conversations drifting from open windows.
It's the kind of thing you don't notice until you've been away and come back.
reactions: Not every place has that. We're lucky.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has twice the market stalls we do. Makes you think.
details: Not that I'm jealous. More selection would be nice, though.
A bigger market means more strangers, more noise, more problems. Maybe what we've got is fine.
reactions: Grass is always greener, I suppose.

entries.intro: I wake up some mornings and just think: I'm glad I ended up in {settlement_name}.
details: It's not perfect, but the people look out for each other. That counts for a lot.
I've lived in places where you didn't know your neighbor's name after a year. This isn't that.
reactions: You don't appreciate a good home until you've had a bad one.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} could use a proper drainage system. Every heavy rain turns the square into a pond.
details: Twice this season I've had water seeping under the door. The threshold needs raising.
People keep talking about fixing it, but talk doesn't dig ditches.
reactions: I'd rather live with a fixable problem than move somewhere with unfixable ones.

statCheck.pass: A graded channel toward the east slope would handle most of it. The grade is already there: just needs digging.
statCheck.fail: I'm no engineer. I just know water shouldn't be where I sleep.

entries.intro: I wonder sometimes what people in {other_settlement} think of {settlement_name}.
details: We probably seem small and quiet to outsiders. That's not necessarily a bad reputation.
The traders who come through always seem comfortable here. That has to mean something.
reactions: We are what we are. No point dressing it up.

entries.intro: There's a smell in {settlement_name} when the wind comes right: woodsmoke and something sweet from the ovens. I never get tired of it.
details: My first morning here, that smell is what made me think I could stay.
You don't notice it after a while, but when you come back from traveling it hits you all at once.
reactions: Home is mostly a feeling. That smell is where mine starts.

entries.intro: If I ever left {settlement_name}, I think I'd miss the mornings most. The way the light comes over the ridge.
details: There's about ten minutes where everything turns gold. The rooftops, the smoke from the chimneys, the water in the troughs.
I've lived here long enough that I almost stopped noticing. Almost.
reactions: You don't realize what makes a place home until you imagine it gone.

entries.intro: Every visitor who comes through {settlement_name} says the same thing: it's smaller than they expected.
details: Then they stay a night, eat a meal, and suddenly they're asking how much a plot of land costs.
Size was never the point. We've got what we need and not much that we don't.
reactions: Let them underestimate us. We know what we've got.

entries.intro: The well in {settlement_name} has needed a new winch for months. Nobody wants to pay for it.
details: Everyone uses it, nobody wants to fund it. That's the eternal problem with shared things.
There was a meeting about it last week that lasted an hour: no decision, typical.
reactions: We'll fix it when the rope finally snaps. That's how decisions get made around here.

statCheck.pass: The real issue is the axle bearing, not the rope. Replace that and the winch would last another five years.
statCheck.fail: I'm no engineer. I just know it squeals when you turn it.

entries.intro: People from outside {settlement_name} always comment on how quiet it is. Like they've never heard silence before.
details: To us it's not quiet at all. I can hear the wind, the animals, someone hammering across the way.
I think they mean there's no crowd noise. No constant background of people doing things they'd rather not.
reactions: I suppose quiet is a luxury if you're not used to it.

entries.intro: There's a time of evening in {settlement_name} when everything just goes quiet. Right before the lamps come on.
details: The work's done, the fires are lit, and for a few minutes nobody's asking anything of anybody.
You can hear the wind through the trees and nothing else. It only lasts a moment.
reactions: That's the part they don't tell you about when they talk about living here. It's the best part.

entries.intro: Outsiders always say {settlement_name} is too quiet. Like that's a complaint.
details: They come through expecting excitement and leave disappointed. Good.
Quiet means nothing's gone wrong. I'll take that over interesting any day.
reactions: They don't understand it because they haven't earned it.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} isn't much to look at. I know that.
details: The buildings lean, the roads are mud half the year, and the well cover doesn't close right.
But every board was put up by someone I know, and every path goes somewhere I've walked a hundred times.
reactions: It's ours. That's enough.

entries.intro: People from {other_settlement} think we don't know how things are done properly in {settlement_name}. We know, we just don't care.
details: They built their roads straighter and their roofs steeper. Good for them.
We built ours the way that works here. Different ground, different weather, different problems.
reactions: Let them think what they want. We're doing fine.

statCheck.pass: Building styles follow local materials and terrain. What works in one place fails in another: that's not ignorance, it's adaptation.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the technical reasons. I just know our way holds up.

entries.intro: You know what I like about {settlement_name}? Nobody pretends to be something they're not.
details: People say what they mean and do what they say. That's rarer than you'd think.
I've been to places where everyone's performing. Here they're just living.
reactions: Honest people make an honest place. That's worth more than fancy buildings.

entries.intro: The roof over the market stall leaks in three places. Been like that since I moved here.
details: Everyone just works around it. Shift your goods a foot to the left and you're fine.
It's become a kind of joke. Somebody put a bucket under the worst spot and it's been there for months.
reactions: Someday we'll fix it. Probably the day after it collapses.

statCheck.pass: The flashing where the roof meets the support beam has pulled away. Seal that joint with pitch and two of the three leaks stop.
statCheck.fail: I don't know roofing. I just know where to put the buckets.

entries.intro: I wouldn't trade {settlement_name} for anywhere. I mean that.
details: It's got its problems, sure. What place doesn't?
But I know every path, every face, every creak in every door. That's home.
reactions: People who keep moving never find what they're looking for. I stopped and it was right here.

entries.intro: The bridge on the east side is getting worse. Boards are soft in the middle.
details: I tested it with my boot last week and felt it give. Not a lot, but enough to notice.
Nobody wants to be the one to close it off because then everyone has to go the long way around.
reactions: We'll deal with it when someone falls through. That's how things work here.

statCheck.pass: The joists underneath are still solid. It's just the decking that needs replacing: pull the old boards and lay new ones in a day.
statCheck.fail: I'm no carpenter. I just know it doesn't feel right underfoot.

entries.intro: I used to live near {other_settlement}. Busier, sure, but busy isn't better.
details: You could barely hear yourself think with all the carts and shouting.
Here in {settlement_name} I can have a thought and finish it.
reactions: Some people need the noise. I needed the quiet.

entries.intro: We need more storage in {settlement_name}. Everyone's cramming things into sheds that are already full.
details: I've got grain stacked next to tools next to firewood. It's a mess.
Twice now someone's lost supplies because there was nowhere dry to keep them.
reactions: It's a growing pain. Means we're doing well enough to have surplus, at least.

entries.intro: Something about the air in {settlement_name} after it rains. Clean and green and alive.
details: Everything smells like wet earth and fresh leaves. Even the mud smells good, somehow.
reactions: Little things like that are what keep you in a place.

entries.intro: I've noticed the paths in {settlement_name} are wearing down faster than we can maintain them.
details: The main path through the center is practically a trench now.
We packed it with gravel last season but the rains washed most of it away.
reactions: You can tell a lot about a place by its paths. Ours need attention.

statCheck.pass: The water's the real culprit. Line the edges with stone so the runoff has somewhere to go instead of cutting through the middle.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the fix. I just know walking through puddles every day is getting old.

entries.intro: I helped build the fence around the east field. Still standing, still straight.
details: We dug those post holes in the heat of summer. Took us three days and we argued about spacing the whole time.
But it held through two bad winters, so I guess we did something right.
reactions: There's something satisfying about looking at a thing you helped make every day.

entries.intro: I'm not saying {settlement_name} is perfect. But I am saying it's ours to fix.
details: Every problem here is a problem we can solve ourselves. We don't need to wait for anyone.
That's not true everywhere. Some places need permission just to move a fence post.
reactions: Self-reliance isn't glamorous, but it's freedom.

entries.intro: The chimney on the old storehouse leans a little more every year. It worries me.
details: Nobody uses the storehouse much anymore, so nobody feels responsible for it.
But if that chimney comes down in a storm, it's going to land on something that matters.
reactions: Prevention costs less than repair. But try telling anyone that.

statCheck.pass: The foundation on the south side has shifted. That's what's pulling the chimney off true. Fix the base and the lean stops.
statCheck.fail: I'm not a mason. I just know leaning is bad.

entries.intro: The sunsets from {settlement_name} are something else. I've never seen them so red anywhere else.
details: It's the way the land falls away to the west. You get this wide open sky and the light just pours across it.
reactions: People pay good money for a view like that. We just step outside.

entries.intro: I hear {other_settlement} has a proper healer now. We're still relying on old remedies here.
details: Not that the old ways don't work. They just take longer and you're never quite sure.
It would be nice to have someone who really knows what they're doing when things go wrong.
reactions: We make do. But making do gets tiring.

entries.intro: What I respect about the people in {settlement_name} is that nobody quits. Things break, we fix them.
details: I've seen the same shed rebuilt twice after storms. Same spot, same people, same stubbornness.
Outsiders would call it foolish. We call it commitment.
reactions: Stubbornness is just dedication without the fancy word.

entries.intro: We don't have a proper gathering hall in {settlement_name}. Just a big room someone's lending.
details: Every meeting we're moving someone's furniture around and putting it back after.
It works, but it's not dignified. Every settlement should have a real place to meet.
reactions: It'll happen eventually. When everyone agrees on where to put it, which could take years.

entries.intro: I know every bird that nests around {settlement_name} by its song. Took me years but I know them all.
details: There's one that only sings right before dawn. I've never even seen it, but I know exactly when it starts.
reactions: That kind of knowing only comes from staying put. It's its own reward.

entries.intro: The water in {settlement_name} tastes better than anywhere I've been. I'll argue about it.
details: Something about the well here. It's cold even in summer and it's got a clean, mineral taste.
I had water in {other_settlement} once and it tasted like mud. Warm mud.
reactions: People take water for granted. Not me.

entries.intro: The fence around the livestock pen has been repaired so many times it's more patches than fence.
details: Every section is a different type of wood from a different year. You can read the history in it.
It holds, barely. One determined goat could probably change that.
reactions: Sometimes I think the fence stays up out of habit.

statCheck.pass: The posts are the weak point. The rails are fine but the posts have rotted at ground level. Replace those and the whole thing tightens up.
statCheck.fail: I'm no fence builder. I just know it wobbles when you lean on it.

entries.intro: Nobody in {settlement_name} locks their doors. I love that about us.
details: You leave something on your porch, it's still there in the morning. Try that in {other_settlement}.
reactions: Trust like that doesn't come from rules. It comes from knowing your neighbors.

entries.intro: The smokehouse needs a new vent. It's been backing up and everything comes out tasting like char.
details: I used to love smoked fish from there. Now it all tastes the same: burned.
Someone mentioned fixing it but nobody's gotten around to it.
reactions: Small problems that nobody fixes become big problems that everybody notices.

statCheck.pass: The flue is probably clogged with creosote. A proper scraping and it'd draw fine again.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what's wrong with it. I just know the food's not right.

entries.intro: I've watched {settlement_name} grow bit by bit. Every new building means someone decided to stay.
details: That's the best compliment a place can get: someone looked around and said this is where I'll be.
reactions: Growth is good as long as we don't lose what made people come in the first place.

entries.intro: Whenever someone from {other_settlement} visits, they always ask where our tavern is.
details: We don't have one. We just drink at each other's houses.
It's less formal and more honest. Also cheaper.
reactions: A tavern would change the feel of {settlement_name}. I'm not sure we need that.

entries.intro: There's a spot on the hill above {settlement_name} where you can see the whole place at once.
details: I go up there sometimes when I need to think. Seeing it all laid out like that calms me down.
You realize how small it is from up there. Small, but complete.
reactions: From a distance, every place looks peaceful. But this one actually is.

entries.intro: The steps by the {poi_type} are crumbling. Someone's going to twist an ankle.
details: I've mentioned it three times now. Everyone agrees it's a problem, nobody agrees whose job it is to fix it.
reactions: I'll probably just do it myself and complain about it for a month.

entries.intro: Children grow up well in {settlement_name}. Room to run, things to climb, people watching out for them.
details: You can let them wander here without worrying. Somebody always has an eye on them.
That's a kind of wealth that doesn't show up when you count coins.
reactions: A good place to raise a family is a good place. Simple as that.

entries.intro: Somebody in {settlement_name} planted flowers along the main path. No idea who.
details: They just appeared one morning. Little white and yellow things poking up between the stones.
Nobody's claimed credit. Everybody waters them when they walk by, though.
reactions: That's the kind of thing that makes a place feel cared for.

entries.intro: The signpost at the crossroads is so faded you can barely read it. Gives a great impression to visitors.
details: Someone scratched directions into the post with a knife as a workaround. That's even worse.
First thing people see arriving at {settlement_name} and it says we don't care enough to repaint a sign.
reactions: It's such an easy fix. That's what makes it annoying.

statCheck.pass: Pine tar mixed with charcoal makes a weather-resistant paint. One afternoon's work and it'd last years.
statCheck.fail: I'm no signmaker. But even I could tell you that one needs redoing.

entries.intro: I like that {settlement_name} doesn't try to impress anyone. What you see is what you get.
details: No painted facades or fancy arches. Just solid walls and roofs that keep the rain out.
reactions: Honest buildings for honest people. I'll take it.

entries.intro: The noise from the {poi_type} carries all the way to my place. Every morning, without fail.
details: I've learned to sleep through most of it, but some mornings it's like they're doing it right outside my window.
I wouldn't ask them to stop: it's their livelihood. I just wish I lived one house further.
reactions: You pick your neighbors when you pick your home. I chose poorly on distance.

entries.intro: When something goes wrong in {settlement_name}, people show up. Without being asked.
details: Roof blows off, there's five people there the next morning with tools. Nobody sends a letter first.
That's not charity, that's just how things work here.
reactions: You can't buy that. You can only build it, slowly, over years.

entries.intro: I think {settlement_name} needs a proper notice board. Right now we just shout things at each other.
details: Half the time I miss announcements because I wasn't in the right place at the right time.
Someone tried nailing a message to a tree once. The rain got it before anyone read it.
reactions: Organization isn't exciting but it makes everything else easier.

entries.intro: I've lived in {settlement_name} long enough to know which boards creak in which building.
details: The third step in the meeting room, the floorboard by the back door, the gate hinge.
People think I'm strange for noticing. I think they're strange for not.
reactions: Knowing a place that well is the definition of home.

entries.intro: The bread in {settlement_name} is better than anything in {other_settlement}. I will die on that hill.
details: It's the flour. Something about the grain grown nearby gives it this nutty taste.
I had bread in {other_settlement} once and it was like chewing a dry sponge.
reactions: I know it sounds small, but good bread makes a good life.

entries.intro: We've got exactly one road into {settlement_name} and it floods every spring.
details: For about two weeks we're cut off from everything. Traders can't get in, we can't get out.
Every year we say we'll fix it. Every year the summer comes and we forget.
reactions: It's tradition at this point. Terrible, wet, muddy tradition.

statCheck.pass: A raised section with a culvert underneath would solve it permanently. The grade's right for water to pass under if you give it a channel.
statCheck.fail: I'm no road builder. I just know I'm tired of getting my boots soaked.

entries.intro: You ever notice how the light hits {settlement_name} in the afternoon? Everything turns warm.
details: The stone walls pick up this golden color and even the dirt looks good.
It only lasts about an hour before the shadows take over. But what an hour.
reactions: Every place has its best angle. That's ours.

entries.intro: The rats in {settlement_name} are getting bolder. I saw one in broad daylight yesterday.
details: Used to be they stayed near the stores and only came out at night. Now they're everywhere.
We need cats or we need traps. Preferably both.
reactions: Nothing makes a place feel unkempt faster than vermin walking around like they pay rent.

statCheck.pass: They're nesting in the grain stores: I've seen the droppings. Seal the gaps at floor level and cut off their food source, and the population drops fast.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where they come from. They're just everywhere all of a sudden.

entries.intro: I measured it once: you can walk from one end of {settlement_name} to the other in about five minutes.
details: That's not a complaint. I like knowing everything is close.
Need something? Walk there, grab it, walk back: no wasted time.
reactions: Big places waste your life on walking. Here you spend it on living.

entries.intro: Somebody needs to clear the brush along the south path. It's halfway to a tunnel now.
details: Branches snag on your clothes, roots trip you up. It was a nice walk a year ago.
I'd do it myself but every time I cut something back it grows in thicker.
reactions: Nature doesn't care about our paths. We have to keep reminding it.

statCheck.pass: Cut at the base, not the tips. Pruning the ends just encourages branching. Take the whole stem down to the root and it stays clear longer.
statCheck.fail: I'm no gardener. I just know whacking at it doesn't help.

entries.intro: The stars over {settlement_name} are something you have to see to believe.
details: No torchlight bleed, no big fires nearby. Just dark sky and more stars than you can count.
I stood outside last night and just looked up for a while. Couldn't help it.
reactions: That's a kind of beauty that costs nothing and never runs out.

entries.intro: I think {settlement_name} is at that awkward size: too big to know everyone, too small to ignore anyone.
details: There are faces I recognize but names I've never learned. That feels wrong somehow.
When I first came here I knew everyone by name inside a week. Can't say that anymore.
reactions: Growing pains, I suppose. Better than shrinking pains.

entries.intro: I've been to {other_settlement} and it's fine, but it's just not {settlement_name}.
details: They've got more shops and better roads, I'll give them that.
But the people don't know each other the way we do. Everyone's polite and nobody's warm.
reactions: Polish without soul is just furniture. I'd rather have a rough table where people actually sit.

entries.intro: The shared tools in {settlement_name} are in terrible shape. Somebody broke the good axe and didn't say anything.
details: I went to borrow it and the head was cracked clean through. Just left hanging on the rack.
That's the problem with shared things: everyone uses them, nobody maintains them.
reactions: If I find out who did it, I won't be angry. I'll just hand them the bill for a new one.

entries.intro: There's a dog in {settlement_name} that belongs to nobody and everybody. Just wanders around being happy.
details: Everyone feeds it scraps, everyone scratches its ears, nobody claims it.
It sleeps wherever it feels like and nobody minds.
reactions: A place that can keep a stray dog happy is doing something right.

entries.intro: We're running low on lamp oil again. Happens every time the days get shorter.
details: By month's end we'll be rationing again and the outer paths will go dark at night.
It's not a disaster, it's just inconvenient in a way that could be avoided with better planning.
reactions: You'd think after living here this long we'd learn to stock up. You'd think wrong.

entries.intro: {npc_name} says we should put up a proper gate for {settlement_name}. I don't see the point.
details: A gate says keep out. We've never needed to say that.
The openness is part of what makes this place feel welcoming.
reactions: Once you put up a gate, you start thinking in terms of who's inside and who's outside. I don't want that.

entries.intro: I like that {settlement_name} is messy. Things aren't lined up and squared off, and it feels real.
details: Buildings face whichever way made sense when they went up. Paths follow the ground, not a plan.
It grew naturally, like a tree. Not like a box somebody drew on paper.
reactions: Perfect places feel empty to me. I like a little chaos.

entries.intro: The well rope snapped last week. We've been hauling water from the stream like it's the old days.
details: It's twice the walk and half the convenience. My arms are sore from carrying buckets.
We've got a new rope on order but nobody knows when it's coming.
reactions: You don't appreciate something until it breaks. Then it's all you think about.

statCheck.pass: The rope frayed where it rubbed on the stone lip. If we smooth that edge or fit a wooden roller, the next rope won't wear through the same way.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it broke. Ropes break, I guess.

entries.intro: Every autumn {settlement_name} smells like apples and woodsmoke. It's the best season here.
details: People bring in the harvest and there's this feeling of plenty. Even if it's modest, it's enough.
The evenings get cool enough for a fire and the cider starts flowing.
reactions: If you visited only once, come in autumn. That's us at our best.

entries.intro: I wish we had someone in {settlement_name} who could work metal properly.
details: Right now anything that needs smithing has to wait for a traveling tinker or a trip to {other_settlement}.
For a broken hinge or a dull blade, that's a lot of waiting.
reactions: Every settlement needs certain skills. We're missing that one.

entries.intro: The quiet here used to bother me. Now it's the thing I'd miss most.
details: When I first arrived in {settlement_name} I thought something was wrong because nothing was happening.
Took me a season to realize nothing happening is the whole point.
reactions: Peace is an acquired taste. But once you've got it, loud places feel unbearable.

entries.intro: The thatch on half the buildings in {settlement_name} is overdue for replacement.
details: You can see daylight through the common hall roof if you look in the right spot.
It's one of those jobs that's too big for one person and too boring for a group.
reactions: We'll do it. Probably right after the first good leak convinces us.

statCheck.pass: If we stagger the work over a few weeks and do two roofs at a time, it's manageable. It's the all-at-once mentality that makes it feel impossible.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the first thing about thatching. I just know rain inside is bad.

entries.intro: I sometimes think about what {settlement_name} will look like in ten years.
details: More buildings, maybe a real market. Or maybe exactly the same, just more weathered.
Either way, I plan to be here to see it.
reactions: Caring about a place's future means you've made it your home.

entries.intro: I heard someone from {other_settlement} called {settlement_name} a backwater. Let them.
details: We sleep well, eat well, and don't owe anyone anything.
If that's a backwater then I'll stay right where I am.
reactions: People who insult small places usually can't manage to build one themselves.

entries.intro: You know what {settlement_name} has that no city does? Silence when you want it.
details: If I want quiet I can walk two minutes in any direction and hear nothing but wind and birds.
Try doing that in a city. You'll walk for an hour and still hear hammering.
reactions: Silence is a resource. We're rich in it.

entries.intro: The latrine situation in {settlement_name} is getting worse. I'll just say that.
details: Too many people, not enough facilities. The pit near the north end is practically full.
Nobody wants to dig a new one because nobody wants to pick the spot.
reactions: Not glamorous, but ignoring it won't make it go away.

statCheck.pass: Dig downhill from the settlement and downwind of the prevailing breeze. Line it with lime after each use and it stays manageable for a long time.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the details. I just know we need a new one somewhere that isn't near my house.

entries.intro: I recognize people in {settlement_name} by their footsteps. That's how well I know this place.
details: {npc_name} drags their left foot a bit, the miller walks fast and heavy, and the kids run everywhere.
When a stranger walks through, I can hear it immediately. The rhythm's wrong.
reactions: Living somewhere long enough turns you into a kind of instrument. You feel the difference.

entries.intro: We grow more than we eat in {settlement_name}. That's something to be proud of.
details: Surplus means we can trade, means we can store for bad seasons, means we're not desperate.
A lot of places can't say that. We can.
reactions: Self-sufficiency isn't exciting. But it's the foundation for everything else.

entries.intro: The common firepit in {settlement_name} is where everything important happens.
details: Decisions, arguments, celebrations, apologies. All in the same circle.
There's something good about facing each other over a fire. Harder to lie that way.
reactions: Every settlement needs a center. Ours is warm.

entries.intro: We don't have a wall around {settlement_name}. Some people think we should.
details: I understand the argument for safety. But a wall changes how a place feels.
Right now you can see the fields from anywhere. A wall cuts that off.
reactions: If the day comes where we need one, we'll build one. Until then, I'd rather see the horizon.

entries.intro: I worry sometimes that the young people won't stay in {settlement_name}.
details: There's not much for them to do here that they haven't done a hundred times already.
The pull of bigger places is strong when you're young and restless.
reactions: I hope a few of them come back. The ones who leave always come back different, but sometimes that's good.

entries.intro: I love that I can hear the rain coming before it arrives in {settlement_name}.
details: The sound rolls across the fields: you hear it hitting the grass before it reaches the rooftops.
Gives you about thirty seconds to get inside. It's like a warning bell made of water.
reactions: You only notice things like that when you're paying attention. This place taught me to pay attention.

entries.intro: The storage cellar under the old building leaks every winter. We've been patching it for years.
details: Half the preserves go soft because of the damp. It's a waste.
Every spring someone says we should dig a new one. By summer nobody remembers saying it.
reactions: We'll keep patching until the floor gives way. Then we'll finally have that hole for the new cellar.

statCheck.pass: The water's coming in through the old foundation seam. A layer of rendered clay on the outside wall would stop it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where the water comes in. I just know it's always wet by February.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} feels different at night. Smaller, closer, like everyone's sharing a room.
details: You can see the lamplight in every window and hear the murmur of conversations from the road.
It's the most connected I ever feel to this place.
reactions: Nighttime is when a settlement stops being buildings and starts being people.

entries.intro: The road to {other_settlement} is in better shape than any path inside {settlement_name}. That's embarrassing.
details: We maintain the road because traders use it. But our own paths are rutted and overgrown.
We treat the way out better than the way home.
reactions: Says something about priorities, doesn't it?

entries.intro: When I first came to {settlement_name}, someone brought me soup before I'd even finished unpacking.
details: Didn't ask my name, didn't ask where I was from. Just handed me a bowl and said welcome.
I've been trying to live up to that kindness ever since.
reactions: That's the kind of place this is. Or at least the kind of place it tries to be.

entries.intro: I wish {settlement_name} had more shade. The center gets brutal in the heat.
details: Whoever planned the layout didn't think about trees. Open ground everywhere.
You end up doing all your errands early morning or late evening just to avoid baking.
reactions: Planting a tree is easy. Getting people to agree where it goes is the hard part.

statCheck.pass: Plant on the west side of the path. Afternoon sun comes from the west, so that's where shade does the most good.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where trees should go. I just know we need them.

entries.intro: I know every shortcut in {settlement_name}. Every gap in every fence, every path between buildings.
details: I can get from one end to the other without using the main road at all.
Kids figured out most of them first. I just followed where they went.
reactions: A place with shortcuts is a place people have been living in long enough to get creative.

entries.intro: There's a tree near the center of {settlement_name} that's been here longer than any building.
details: Big old thing with a trunk so wide two people can't reach around it.
People sit under it, argue under it, rest under it. It's the closest thing we have to a landmark.
reactions: A settlement grows around certain things. That tree is one of ours.

entries.intro: Firewood distribution in {settlement_name} is a mess. Some people hoard, some people scramble.
details: There's no system. Whoever chops the most keeps the most.
Means the elderly and the busy always end up short when the cold hits.
reactions: We need a fair split. I've said it before and I'll say it again.

statCheck.pass: A communal woodlot with a rotation: each household takes a set amount per week based on size. Fair, trackable, and nobody hoards.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to organize it. I just know the current way isn't fair.

entries.intro: People here help each other without keeping score. That's rare.
details: I fixed {npc_name}'s fence last month and they brought me eggs this week. Neither of us mentioned the other thing.
It's not transactional. It's just how {settlement_name} works.
reactions: The moment people start counting favors, a community starts dying.

entries.intro: The wind comes from the wrong direction in {settlement_name} sometimes and brings the smell of the compost heap.
details: On those days you can't sit outside without gagging. It's eye-watering.
Everyone's used to it but newcomers always look horrified.
reactions: I think of it as the smell of future harvests. Makes it slightly less awful.

entries.intro: I think what makes {settlement_name} work is that nobody's trying to be in charge.
details: Decisions get made by whoever cares enough to show up and argue about it.
It's inefficient, sure. But nobody feels ruled, and that matters.
reactions: The best leadership is the kind nobody notices.

entries.intro: Traders always look surprised when they get to {settlement_name}. I think they expect more.
details: We're on the maps, but we're not exactly a destination. More of a stop on the way to somewhere else.
Still, they always sell something and buy something. We're worth the stop.
reactions: Being underestimated has its advantages. People leave their expectations behind.

entries.intro: We've got a problem with the drainage ditch near the {poi_type}. It's silted up completely.
details: Water just pools there now. Mosquitoes love it, everyone else hates it.
Clearing it would take a day of hard work. Finding a day when everyone can do it is the real challenge.
reactions: One day of work to fix a season of misery. You'd think we'd just do it.

statCheck.pass: If we line the bottom with gravel after clearing it, the silt won't build up as fast. Might only need clearing every other year.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it keeps filling up. Ditches just do that, I guess.

entries.intro: I've slept better since moving to {settlement_name} than anywhere else in my life.
details: Something about the air here. Or maybe it's just knowing I'm safe.
I used to wake up three times a night. Here I sleep straight through.
reactions: Good sleep is worth more than gold. I found a gold mine and didn't even know it.

entries.intro: The walls in {settlement_name} are getting thin. I can hear my neighbor snore.
details: Not much privacy when the buildings are packed this close together.
I know more about my neighbor's habits than I ever wanted to.
reactions: Community is great until you can hear it through the wall at midnight.

statCheck.pass: A layer of packed straw between the inner and outer wall would cut the sound in half. Cheap and easy if you do it during a repair.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to make walls thicker. I just know mine aren't thick enough.

entries.intro: People in {other_settlement} feel like strangers to each other even though they live side by side.
details: I visited once and nobody said hello. Not on the path, not at the well, nowhere.
In {settlement_name}, ignoring someone is practically an insult.
reactions: I'd rather live in a place that's too friendly than one that's too polite.

entries.intro: The wind in {settlement_name} is relentless sometimes. Gets in through every crack.
details: I've stuffed cloth in gaps, hung blankets over the door. The wind finds a way.
On bad nights it whistles through the eaves like it's trying to have a conversation.
reactions: You learn to live with what you can't fix. The wind is one of those.

statCheck.pass: Most of the draft comes from where the wall meets the roof plate. Daubing that seam with clay mixed with horsehair would block the worst of it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where the drafts come from. Everywhere, it feels like.

entries.intro: There's a spot by the {poi_type} where the morning sun comes through just right. I sit there when I can.
details: Warm on your face, quiet everywhere. Five minutes there and the day feels manageable.
reactions: Everyone should have a spot like that. Mine happens to be here in {settlement_name}.

entries.intro: I think about leaving {settlement_name} sometimes. Then I think about where I'd go, and I've got no answer.
details: Every other place is either too big, too far, or too different.
The fact that nowhere else appeals tells me something.
reactions: Staying isn't giving up. It's choosing.

entries.intro: The nights are so dark in {settlement_name} when the lamps go out. I love it.
details: Real darkness. The kind where you have to trust your feet because your eyes are useless.
It scared me at first. Now I find it restful.
reactions: Darkness isn't absence. It's quiet for the eyes.

entries.intro: The soil near {settlement_name} is heavy clay. Makes everything harder than it needs to be.
details: Digging a post hole takes three times as long. The gardens struggle if you don't amend the soil.
And when it rains, the clay holds the water and everything turns to a slippery mess.
reactions: We work with what the land gives us. The land here is not generous.

statCheck.pass: Mixing in sand and compost breaks up the clay over time. It's a lot of labor upfront but the soil improves each year.
statCheck.fail: I'm no farmer. I just know my shovel bounces off the ground half the time.

entries.intro: I know every roof in {settlement_name} from below. The patches, the moss, the bird nests.
details: You walk the same paths enough times and you start looking up instead of ahead.
There's a whole world up there that most people never notice.
reactions: Familiarity doesn't have to mean boredom. It can mean depth.

entries.intro: Someone in {settlement_name} keeps a garden that puts the rest of us to shame.
details: Flowers, herbs, vegetables, all in neat rows. It looks like it belongs in a painting.
Meanwhile my yard looks like a battlefield. Some people just have the touch.
reactions: A beautiful garden lifts the whole settlement. Even if the rest of us can't manage one.

entries.intro: The morning fog in {settlement_name} makes the whole place look like a dream.
details: Buildings fade in and out as you walk. Voices carry but you can't tell where from.
It burns off by midmorning, but for a while everything feels soft and uncertain.
reactions: I never get tired of it. Same fog, different feeling every time.

entries.intro: We still don't have a proper spot to wash clothes in {settlement_name}. Everyone uses the stream.
details: In winter that means frozen fingers and stiff fabric. It's miserable.
A simple wash basin with a roof over it would change everything.
reactions: Basic things matter more than fancy things. We're missing some basics.

statCheck.pass: Divert a trickle from the stream into a stone basin near the square. Running water, covered, central. It's not complicated, just labor.
statCheck.fail: I'm no builder. I just know the stream is too far and too cold.

entries.intro: I've watched three different families settle in {settlement_name} this year. That feels like a good sign.
details: People are choosing to come here. Not because they have to, but because they want to.
New faces take getting used to, but new energy is always welcome.
reactions: A growing settlement is a living settlement.

entries.intro: The arguments in {settlement_name} are always about the same three things: water, paths, and fences.
details: Every meeting circles back to one of those eventually. You could set a clock by it.
We never fully resolve any of them. We just argue until everyone's tired and go home.
reactions: At least we argue. Places that stop arguing have given up.

entries.intro: There's a sound {settlement_name} makes when everyone's working. A rhythm of tools and voices.
details: Hammers, saws, the scrape of a hoe, someone calling out to someone else.
It all blends together into this hum. It's the sound of a place being alive.
reactions: I'd rather hear that than any music.

entries.intro: I keep saying we need a second well in {settlement_name}. The line at the first one is ridiculous.
details: Peak morning you're waiting half an hour with a bucket. That's time nobody has.
But digging a well is expensive and nobody can agree where it should go.
reactions: We'll get there slowly, like everything here.

statCheck.pass: The west side has the best water table: I've seen the ground stay damp there even in dry seasons. That's where to dig.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where the water is underground. I just know we need more of it up here.

entries.intro: The first frost in {settlement_name} is always a shock, even when you know it's coming.
details: Everything goes white overnight and the air bites your lungs. The whole place looks different.
But the fires get stoked and people check on each other more. Cold brings people closer.
reactions: Seasons keep {settlement_name} honest. You can't get complacent when winter's always coming.

entries.intro: {npc_name} has been talking about starting a workshop in {settlement_name}. I hope they do.
details: We've needed skilled work done locally for ages. Everything gets sent out or jury-rigged.
If someone with actual skill set up here, they'd never run out of customers.
reactions: One good craftsperson changes a whole settlement. I've seen it happen.

entries.intro: I know it's silly but I get defensive when people criticize {settlement_name}.
details: I'll criticize it all day long myself, but the moment an outsider does it I bristle.
It's like family. You can call your brother an idiot but nobody else gets to.
reactions: That's how you know you belong somewhere. You're allowed to complain and offended when others do.

entries.intro: We lost a big section of the boundary fence in the last storm. Still hasn't been repaired.
details: The livestock keep wandering and someone has to round them up every evening.
It's turned into a daily chore that didn't exist two weeks ago.
reactions: Storm damage is nobody's fault, but leaving it unfixed is everybody's fault.

statCheck.pass: The posts held: it's the cross-members that snapped. Peg new rails to the existing posts and you've got a fence again in half a day.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure how to fix it. I just know the goats keep ending up in my garden.

entries.intro: I came to {settlement_name} planning to stay one season. That was years ago.
details: Something about this place just grabs you. Not dramatically, not all at once.
It's the slow kind of belonging. One day you realize you've stopped thinking about leaving.
reactions: The best things in life sneak up on you.

entries.intro: The food stores in {settlement_name} are barely organized. Grain next to lamp oil next to dried meat.
details: Anyone who goes in there looking for something specific has to dig through everything else first.
Mice got into the flour last month because it was stored at floor level. Preventable.
reactions: Organization is nobody's job, so it's nobody's priority. And we all pay for it.

statCheck.pass: Raised shelving and sealed bins would fix most of it. Keep grain off the floor and away from strong-smelling goods. It's basic storeroom sense.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to organize a storeroom. I just know ours is a mess.

entries.intro: What I appreciate about {settlement_name} is the pace. Nothing moves faster than it needs to.
details: People walk, they don't rush. Work gets done when it gets done.
In bigger places everyone's frantic and nobody's any happier for it.
reactions: Speed is overrated. Steadiness is what builds things that last.

entries.intro: There's a crack in the wall of the common building that's been there since before I arrived.
details: Everyone walks past it every day. It's become invisible.
One winter it's going to spread and then we'll have a real problem instead of a cosmetic one.
reactions: We're very good at ignoring things that haven't become emergencies yet.

statCheck.pass: It's following the mortar line, which means it's the binding failing, not the stone. Re-point the joints and it'll hold another decade.
statCheck.fail: I'm not a mason. I just know cracks get bigger, never smaller.

entries.intro: I've been to enough places to know that {settlement_name} has something rare: contentment.
details: People here aren't chasing something they don't have. They're tending what they do.
That's not laziness or lack of ambition. It's wisdom.
reactions: Ambition built cities, but contentment built communities. I know which one I prefer.

entries.intro: I'm told {other_settlement} has paved roads. Must be nice not to track mud everywhere.
details: On wet days here you can trace where everyone's been by the footprints. Into every building, across every threshold.
We live in a permanent negotiation with dirt.
reactions: Paved roads are a luxury. Clean boots are a fantasy.

entries.intro: Cooking smells in {settlement_name} around dinnertime are the best part of my evening walk.
details: Every house has something different going. Stew here, bread there, something roasting at the end.
You can walk the whole settlement and eat with your nose.
reactions: A place that cooks together lives together. Even if it's in separate kitchens.

entries.intro: We get more wind than {other_settlement} does. You can always tell when you're approaching {settlement_name}: your hat tries to leave.
details: Something about the terrain funnels it right through here. Open ground on both sides.
Good for drying laundry. Terrible for everything else.
reactions: You adapt. Plant low, build sturdy, hold onto your things.

entries.intro: I trust the people in {settlement_name} with my life. Not a phrase I use lightly.
details: When my roof leaked last winter, three neighbors were there before I even asked. Worked through the cold to fix it.
reactions: You can live somewhere for years and not trust anyone. Or you can live here.

entries.intro: The timber we've been getting for repairs is green and warps as it dries. Everything we build with it twists.
details: Doors that closed fine in summer won't shut by winter. Frames pull away from walls.
We need properly seasoned wood but nobody wants to pay the difference.
reactions: Cheap now costs more later. We never seem to learn that.

statCheck.pass: If we cut timber in autumn and let it season through winter under cover, we'd have usable wood by spring. It just takes planning a season ahead.
statCheck.fail: I don't know much about wood. I just know our doors don't close.

entries.intro: I like watching {settlement_name} wake up in the morning. One light at a time.
details: First the baker, then the ones with animals, then slowly everyone else.
By the time I'm outside, there's smoke from every chimney and the day's already moving.
reactions: A settlement waking up is the most hopeful thing I know.

entries.intro: Gossip travels so fast in {settlement_name} it might as well be the wind.
details: I sneezed at breakfast yesterday and three people asked if I was sick before noon.
There are no secrets here. That's a blessing and a curse.
reactions: Privacy is the one luxury we genuinely don't have.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} is the kind of place where someone saves you a seat without you asking.
details: You show up and there's a spot. Always has been, always will be.
That's not a small thing. That's someone thinking about you when you're not there.
reactions: Belonging isn't a big dramatic moment. It's a saved seat.

entries.intro: The chickens in {settlement_name} roam wherever they please. It's charming until you step in something.
details: Nobody claims them, nobody confines them. They're a shared inconvenience.
I've chased them out of my doorway more times than I can count.
reactions: Free-range is a polite word for unsupervised.

entries.intro: I walked through {other_settlement} last month and everything looked perfect. Too perfect.
details: Straight paths, matching buildings, not a thing out of place. Like a painting.
But nobody was talking to each other. Everyone just passed by with their heads down.
reactions: I'll take our crooked little {settlement_name} over a pretty cage any day.

entries.intro: We're overdue for a good cleaning in {settlement_name}. Leaves and debris everywhere.
details: The corners pile up with rubbish nobody claims. The gutter by the main path is half-clogged.
It's not filthy, but it's not tidy either. We can do better.
reactions: One good day of everyone pitching in would transform this place. Just one.

statCheck.pass: Start with the gutters. Blocked drainage causes half the other problems: standing water, mud, rot. Clear those and the rest follows naturally.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know where to start. Maybe everywhere at once.

entries.intro: When the wind is calm, the smoke from all the chimneys in {settlement_name} just hangs in the air like a blanket.
details: It settles into a layer above the rooftops. You can see it from the fields: a flat gray line.
Smells like home though. Even if it makes my eyes water.
reactions: Beauty and nuisance live side by side here. Like everything else.

entries.intro: Everyone in {settlement_name} has an opinion on how things should be done. Every last person.
details: Ask five people how to fix a fence and you'll get six answers.
It's maddening sometimes. But it means people care enough to argue.
reactions: A place full of opinionated people is a place that's paying attention.

entries.intro: I've eaten meals in every home in {settlement_name}. Not by invitation, just by being around at the right time.
details: If you're nearby when food's ready, you eat. That's the rule.
Nobody keeps track, nobody expects repayment. The food just flows.
reactions: A settlement that feeds its people without counting is a wealthy one, whatever the coin says.

entries.intro: The woodpile behind the common hall is a disaster. One good shove and it's all coming down.
details: People just throw logs on top without stacking properly. It leans worse every day.
I tried restacking it last week and gave up after the third collapse.
reactions: It's a metaphor for {settlement_name}, honestly. Held together by stubbornness and hope.

statCheck.pass: Alternate the direction of each layer: north-south, then east-west. Creates a self-bracing structure that won't lean.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the trick to stacking wood. Mine always falls over too.

entries.intro: I'll say this for {settlement_name}: nobody here goes hungry. Nobody.
details: If someone's short on food, their neighbor covers them. No questions, no shame.
I've been on both ends of that. Neither one feels like charity.
reactions: That's not a policy. That's just who we are.

entries.intro: The bugs in {settlement_name} in summer are unbelievable. I've never been bitten this much anywhere.
details: Standing water everywhere breeds them and the evening air is thick with them.
I go inside at dusk and don't come out until they settle. Which is basically dawn.
reactions: Every place has a downside. Ours has wings and drinks blood.

statCheck.pass: They breed in the standing water by the ditches. Drain those pools or stock them with fish and the swarms drop to nothing within a week.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to stop them. Short of moving somewhere cold.

entries.intro: There's something about coming back to {settlement_name} after being away. Even a short trip.
details: You see the rooftops from the road and something in your chest loosens.
It's relief, I think. The kind of relief that means you've found where you're supposed to be.
reactions: Home isn't where you were born. It's where you relax.

entries.intro: Half the shutters in {settlement_name} don't close properly. Mine included.
details: They were fine when they were hung. Weather and settling warped every frame.
Now they flap in the wind at night and you just learn to sleep through it.
reactions: Add it to the list of things we'll fix someday.

statCheck.pass: The hinges have rusted and the pins are swelling. Replace the pins with wooden pegs and they'll swing true again.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they stick. They just do.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} doesn't ask much of you. Just be decent and pull your weight.
details: No oaths, no dues, no complicated rules. Just be a good neighbor.
That's harder than it sounds, but the bar is clear.
reactions: Simple expectations make for honest relationships.

entries.intro: The rooster situation in {settlement_name} is out of control. Three of them now, all competing.
details: They start before dawn and they don't stop. It's like a screaming contest nobody wins.
I've started sleeping with cloth over my ears.
reactions: Two roosters too many. But try telling that to the people who own them.

entries.intro: I think the best thing about {settlement_name} is that mistakes are forgiven here.
details: People mess up, they apologize, life goes on. Nobody holds it over your head forever.
That kind of grace doesn't happen by accident. It's a choice this place keeps making.
reactions: You can rebuild trust in a small place. In a big one it just gets forgotten.

entries.intro: The meeting last week about the {poi_type} went nowhere. Three hours of talking in circles.
details: Everyone agrees it needs attention. Nobody agrees on what kind.
We'll meet again next month and have the exact same conversation.
reactions: Democracy is wonderful until you need something done quickly.

entries.intro: Somebody carved a little face into the post by the well in {settlement_name}. Nobody knows who.
details: It's just a simple thing: two dots and a curve. But it makes me smile every time I draw water.
reactions: The best parts of a place are the ones nobody planned.

entries.intro: We've been talking about building a second path out of {settlement_name} for as long as I've lived here.
details: One way in and one way out makes everything slower. Traders, travelers, emergencies.
But the terrain to the south is rocky and nobody wants to do the work.
reactions: The longer we wait, the more we'll wish we hadn't.

statCheck.pass: There's a natural grade along the southeast that follows an old animal trail. Half the clearing work is already done by hooves.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know where to start. I just know one road isn't enough.

entries.intro: There's a rhythm to life in {settlement_name} that I've grown into. It fits me now.
details: I know when the water's best, when the paths are dry, when the market has fresh stock.
My life and this place are synced up. I don't have to think about the pattern anymore.
reactions: That's comfort. Not the lazy kind: the earned kind.

entries.intro: I wish people in {settlement_name} would stop dumping ash behind their buildings. It blows everywhere.
details: My clean laundry ended up gray last week because the wind carried someone's firepit cleanings across the whole row.
A shared ash pit would solve it. But getting people to walk that far with a bucket is apparently asking too much.
reactions: Shared problems need shared solutions. We haven't figured that out yet.

statCheck.pass: Wet the ash before dumping it. Damp ash doesn't blow. Problem solved with half a bucket of water.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to keep ash from blowing. I just know it ends up on my clothes.

entries.intro: If {settlement_name} was a person, it'd be the kind who doesn't say much but is always there when it counts.
details: Not flashy, not loud. Just steady and real.
reactions: I've met a lot of flashy places. None of them made me want to stay.

entries.intro: The geese near the pond in {settlement_name} have gotten territorial. They hiss at anyone who walks by.
details: I saw one chase {npc_name} halfway down the path. Full sprint, wings out.
They act like they own the place. And honestly, nobody's challenged them on it.
reactions: We've been conquered by birds and we just accept it.

entries.intro: I measure time by what's happening in {settlement_name}. Planting season, mending season, harvest, rest.
details: I don't know what day of the week it is half the time. I know what needs doing.
The land sets the calendar here. We just follow it.
reactions: There's something honest about living by the seasons instead of by the page.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} lost three families this year. People just packed up and left.
details: That doesn't happen in {settlement_name}. People arrive, they stay.
Whatever we're doing right, I hope we keep doing it.
reactions: Retention is the truest measure of a place. We pass that test.

entries.intro: The ground around the {poi_type} stays muddy year-round. Something's not draining right.
details: You have to plan your route through there carefully or you sink in up to your ankle.
People have started laying planks down, which means it's officially gone from a nuisance to a landmark.
reactions: When people name a problem instead of fixing it, you know it's become permanent.

statCheck.pass: There's probably a spring seep under the surface. A shallow trench to divert it would dry the area out within a season.
statCheck.fail: No idea why it's always wet. Ground just does what it wants, I suppose.

entries.intro: My favorite sound in {settlement_name} is the creak of the gate when people come home in the evening.
details: It's the same creak every time. Familiar, predictable, safe.
When I hear it I know the day is wrapping up and everyone's accounted for.
reactions: Home sounds are the sounds you'd record if you knew you were leaving.

entries.intro: We really need to sort out the boundary between the gardens and the common area.
details: Every season someone's plants creep a little further into shared space. It's been happening for years.
Nobody says anything because nobody wants to start that argument.
reactions: Polite avoidance isn't the same as solving a problem. We're experts at the first one.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} is proof you don't need much to live well. Roof, food, good neighbors.
details: I've seen wealthy people in miserable places and poor people in happy ones.
What you have around you matters more than what you have in your pocket.
reactions: Simplicity gets a bad reputation from people who've never tried it.
